name: per-burrow-pqueue-with-global-cap
description: Run loop uses one PQueue(concurrency=1) per burrowId for FIFO ordering of runs against the same burrow, wrapped by a single PQueue(concurrency=globalConcurrency, default 8) that bounds how many burrows are driven in parallel. RunsRepo.claimById(runId) does an atomic queued→running transition inside a SQLite transaction so a doubly-enqueued runId can't be claimed twice. AbortController per-burrow lets stop({force:true}) signal in-flight handlers; finalize-on-throw turns a handler exception into a 'failed' run instead of silently leaking. Phase 4 plugs in the real handler that drives BurrowProvider + AgentRuntime; Phase 2 keeps it injectable for testability.

name: in-process-run-dispatcher
description: burrow serve boots a RunDispatcher (src/runner/dispatcher.ts) alongside the HTTP listener: a single RunLoop with handler=dispatchRun (src/runner/dispatch.ts), wired to RunsClient.setOnCreated so HTTP-enqueued runs flow into the loop the instant they're inserted. Loop boots BEFORE startServer (recovery sweep + create hook installed before first request). Stop order on shutdown is HTTP first, dispatcher second with force=true. Pre-fix (burrow-7b97), startServer never instantiated an executor; HTTP-enqueued runs sat at state=queued indefinitely. Library consumers that don't run a server can skip startRunDispatcher; the Client itself stays unchanged.

content: dispatchRun (src/runner/dispatch.ts) ALWAYS returns RunOutcome — never throws on infra failures (spawn, proxy, prepareWorkspace). Pre-fix prompt.ts threw on spawn failures; post-fix the failure is encoded as outcome.errorMessage so the daemon RunLoop path doesn't have to wrap a try/catch. Caller is responsible for runs.finalize(claimed.id, outcome) — dispatchRun assumes the run is already claimed (state=running) and never writes the terminal state itself. messagesDelivered count surfaces back via opts.onMessagesClaimed callback.

Dispatcher end-to-end tests for a runtime (e.g. piRuntime in src/runner/dispatcher.test.ts) register the real AgentRuntime, replay the parser-layer golden fixtures (src/runtime/parsers/__golden__/*.jsonl) via fakeSpawn({stdoutLines}), override installCheck via the startRunDispatcher option (so the host doesn't need the binary), and assert persistence fidelity by computing expected = fixtureLines.flatMap(parseXEvents) and comparing against client.repos.events.listByBurrow(...).filter(runId). The parser tests already cover envelope-by-envelope mapping; the dispatcher test's job is the spawn→parse→persist bridge (argv contract, stdin shape, ordered DB roundtrip). Note that EventRow exposes the persisted payload as row.payloadJson (not .payload).

Dispatcher (src/runner/dispatch.ts) calls runtime.extractMetadata({burrow, run, workspacePath}) only when the agent exited with code 0. The returned patch (if any keys) goes through repos.runs.patchMetadata. Extraction errors are swallowed — best-effort; failing extraction never fails an otherwise-successful run. Cancellation and non-zero exits skip the hook entirely so we don't persist garbage from interrupted runs.

description: Pi e2e dispatcher tests (src/runner/dispatcher.test.ts) timed out (waitFor 1000ms) after piRuntime gained prepareWorkspace because seedActiveBurrow used '/ws' as workspacePath. mkdirSync(/ws/.pi/sessions, recursive:true) failed (no host permission to /), the run finalized failed, and the test waiting for state==='succeeded' hung. The error was hidden because run-loop.ts wraps handler exceptions into a generic finalize({state:'failed', errorMessage}).
resolution: Make seedActiveBurrow accept an explicit workspacePath and pass a per-test mkdtempSync path for any test that exercises a runtime with prepareWorkspace / extractMetadata. The fake spawn replaces the binary, but the workspace itself must be a real host dir.

content: Dispatcher mid-run steering loop (src/runner/dispatch.ts runMidRunSteeringLoop, SPEC §13.5) polls repos.messages.listPending(burrow.id) on a 200ms tick (input.midRunInboxPollMs override) while stdin is held. Per pending row: encode via runtime.encodeSteeringMessage, await proc.writeStdin(stdin), then repos.messages.markDelivered(msg.id, runId) and appendAndPublish an 'inbox_delivered' system event with payload {messageId, priority, mode:'mid_run'}. writeStdin reject → return immediately leaving the row unread (next-spawn delivers); the loop is aborted in dispatchRun's finally BEFORE the defensive closeStdin so a final tick can't race teardown. Only active when useStdinHold AND runtime.encodeSteeringMessage AND proc.writeStdin are all present.

Cross-process e2e for burrow serve dispatcher (burrow-e2bc, pl-cb3e step 1) spawns a sibling Bun subprocess via a fixture (src/server/dispatcher-cross-process.fixture.ts) that imports the library directly and registers an in-process fake AgentRuntime + SpawnFn before calling runServeCommand. The parent test (src/server/dispatcher-cross-process.test.ts) pre-migrates SQLite via an in-test Client, seeds a burrow row, POSTs /burrows/:id/runs over TCP, then polls runs.tryGet() until terminal — SQLite WAL provides the cross-process visibility. Counterpart to the in-process e2e at src/cli/commands/serve.test.ts:285; locks burrow-7b97's RunsClient.setOnCreated -> RunDispatcher wiring for warren's cross-host fan-out.

Test-only library fixtures (e.g. src/server/dispatcher-cross-process.fixture.ts) follow the naming convention '*.fixture.ts' — they are NOT *.test.ts so bun test skips them, they ship under src/ (visible to TypeScript + lint), and they keep their first line as '#!/usr/bin/env bun' for direct invocation. Tests spawn them via 'process.execPath run <fixture>'; the parent reads a one-line JSON banner off stdout to discover the bound port, then SIGTERMs the subprocess after assertions.

RunDispatcherHandle.drain (DrainController interface, pl-cb3e step 4 / burrow-79ad) is the single source of truth for the worker-draining bit: lives on the dispatcher (src/runner/dispatcher.ts) so it survives across multiple HTTP listeners on the same worker. Plain mutable boolean — no events fire on change; the next HTTP request observes the new value via isDraining(). The dispatcher itself is oblivious to the flag (still drives queued and in-flight runs to terminal); only the HTTP layer (createBurrow + createRun handlers, wrapped via withDrainGate in src/server/admin.ts) consults it. Wired in production by runServeCommand passing {admin: {drain: dispatcher.drain}} to startServer.

dispatchRun (src/runner/dispatch.ts) routes resume runs: when run.resumeOfRunId is set it validates eligibility BEFORE the inbox claim (so a rejected resume never consumes pending messages) and fails fast as a structured failed RunOutcome (never throws) for four modes: runtime lacks buildResumeCommand, prior run missing, prior run in a different burrow, or prior run non-terminal (RUN_TERMINAL_STATES). On success it calls runtime.buildResumeCommand({...spawnCtx, priorRun}) instead of buildSpawnCommand. Tested in dispatcher.test.ts; the non-terminal test must start() the dispatcher BEFORE seeding the running prior or the startup recovery sweep flips it to failed (terminal).

name: destroy-drains-runloop-before-prune
description: burrow destroy coordinates with the RunLoop before pruning (burrow-4855). BurrowsClient.setOnDestroy registers RunLoop.drainBurrow(id,{force:true}); destroyBurrowFully stops the burrow, awaits drainRuns(id) to abort+drain in-flight runs (tearing down their sandboxes), then removes workspace + prunes rows. Paired with RunsRepo.finalize now returning RunRow|null (no NotFoundError) so RunLoop.execute tolerates a row pruned mid-flight instead of crashing with 'run not found at finalize'.
